candidates.name: Colleen Melody
candidates.one_liner: Career civil rights lawyer appointed by Gov. Ferguson, who defended the capital gains tax as Attorney General. Her side has the strongest reasons to want a state income tax.
candidates.prior_practice: Civil Rights Division Chief at the WA Attorney General's Office. About 20 years in civil rights enforcement.
candidates.endorsements: Gov. Ferguson; sitting WA Supreme Court justices; King County Bar.

signals.type: Who put her on the court
signals.text: Gov. Bob Ferguson appointed her in November 2025. As Attorney General, Ferguson defended the capital gains tax all the way to the state Supreme Court.
Career civil rights lawyer. About 20 years prosecuting discrimination cases for the state. No tax law in her resume.

signals.type: How she talks about the law
signals.text: Campaign materials emphasize equity and access to justice. She has cited Justice Elena Kagan as an influence. She does not describe herself as a textualist.

signals.type: What she has said about Culliton
signals.text: Nothing directly. No published opinions, articles, or campaign statements on the 1933 ruling.

questions: How would you approach a court ruling that's nearly a century old but is widely seen as legally shaky?
When the words of the constitution and a long-standing interpretation point in different directions, which should win?

candidates.name: Scott Edwards
candidates.current_role: Partner at Ballard Spahr; UW Law affiliate faculty
candidates.one_liner: Tax lawyer who has spent his career fighting the Department of Revenue on behalf of taxpayers. People in his world tend to like the rule that blocks an income tax.
candidates.appointed_by: Not applicable. He's a challenger, not a sitting judge.
candidates.prior_practice: Tax lawyer. Represents companies and individuals in disputes with the Washington Department of Revenue.

signals.text: He sues the state tax agency for a living. His client base benefits from the current setup where Washington has no income tax.

signals.type: How he talks about the law
signals.text: Campaign focuses on rule of law, a predictable tax system, and fairness for taxpayers.
Teaches state and local tax at UW Law. He knows Article VII and Culliton inside and out — this isn't theoretical for him.

signals.type: What he has said about Culliton
signals.text: No public position on overturning it specifically. His professional incentives strongly suggest he'd preserve it.

questions: After 90 years, should the court revisit how it defined property in the tax context?
What's the judge's job when the legislature passes a tax whose constitutionality is in doubt?

candidates.name: J. Michael Diaz
candidates.one_liner: Former federal prosecutor appointed by Gov. Inslee, who signed Washington's capital gains tax. Less direct signal than other appointees, but he's in the same coalition.
candidates.prior_practice: Judge on the WA Court of Appeals. Before that, federal prosecutor and US Department of Justice Civil Rights Division.
candidates.endorsements: Sitting WA Supreme Court justices; bar association ratings strong.

signals.type: Who put him on the court
signals.text: Gov. Jay Inslee appointed him. Inslee signed Washington's capital gains tax and his administration defended it in court.
Career federal prosecutor and civil rights enforcement. Limited experience with state tax law specifically.

signals.type: How he talks about the law
signals.text: Public writings emphasize careful legal reasoning, plain meaning, and the institutional credibility of courts. More measured than ideological.

signals.type: What he has said about Culliton
signals.text: Nothing direct. His Court of Appeals record doesn't include cases squarely on this topic.

questions: How does federal precedent influence how you think about long-standing state Supreme Court rulings?
When the constitution's words and a 90-year-old interpretation diverge, which should govern?

candidates.name: David Stevens
candidates.one_liner: Self-described textualist who looks to Scalia for guidance. Counterintuitively, that approach actually preserves the 1933 ruling rather than overturning it.
candidates.prior_practice: Trial court judge in Mason County. Private practice before that.
candidates.endorsements: WA Republican Party recommendation; local bar and law enforcement.

signals.type: How he talks about the law
signals.text: Cites US Justices Scalia and Alito as influences. Emphasizes constitution as written textualism.
If you read Article VII strictly as written and stick with how the 1933 court understood property, you keep the rule. Stevens's methodology entrenches it.
Backed by Republican-aligned judicial infrastructure. His coalition has built its tax politics around the existing rule.
Trial court judges don't write Article VII opinions. The signal here is methodology and coalition, not case record.

questions: If the original public meaning of property in 1889 was different from what the 1933 court said, which controls?
How should a textualist approach a ruling that has shaped tax policy for nine decades?

candidates.name: Ian Birk
candidates.one_liner: Plaintiff-side lawyer turned judge. Strong progressive endorsement slate. Same coalition that wants a graduated income tax — but his own record on this is thin.
candidates.appointed_by: Gov. Jay Inslee (to the Court of Appeals)
candidates.prior_practice: Civil rights and constitutional litigation in Seattle, on the plaintiffs' side.
candidates.endorsements: Heavy slate of progressive elected officials and labor unions.

signals.type: Who put him on the bench
signals.text: Inslee appointed him to the Court of Appeals — same governor who signed the capital gains tax.
Spent his career suing on behalf of plaintiffs in civil rights cases. Comfortable with constitutional litigation.
Endorsement list tilts heavily progressive and pro-labor relative to other Position 4 candidates.
No Court of Appeals opinion directly on Article VII or Culliton. He hasn't had a chance to show his hand on this.

questions: When a ruling has limited the legislature's tax authority for decades, how much weight does that history get?
What's the line between fixing a legal mistake and judges making policy?

candidates.name: Sean O'Donnell
candidates.one_liner: Career prosecutor turned trial judge. Cross-partisan endorsements. He hasn't said much that would tip his hand either way.
candidates.prior_practice: King County Deputy Prosecuting Attorney. Long criminal trial career before the bench.
candidates.endorsements: Cross-partisan: law enforcement, prosecutors, bar associations.

signals.text: Career prosecutor. Trial-court judge, not appellate. His instincts are courtroom and case management, not doctrine.

signals.type: How he talks about the law
signals.text: No clear ideological signature. Moderate King County judicial style.
Cross-partisan endorsement pattern typical of moderate candidates. Doesn't tell you much about how he'd rule on tax cases.
Nothing public on Article VII or Culliton. Genuine uncertainty.

questions: How does a trial court background shape how you weigh real-world disruption when considering whether to overturn precedent?
Which kinds of cases should the court be most cautious about reaching?

candidates.name: David Shelvey
candidates.one_liner: Filed to run but appears to have no real campaign. No fundraising, no endorsements, no public statements. Included for completeness.
candidates.fundraising: PDC reports $0 raised as of latest filing.

signals.text: No campaign infrastructure, no fundraising, no public endorsements located. May not be a serious contender.
Solo private practice. No published opinions or articles found.
Nothing located.

questions: Why are you seeking this seat, and what experience qualifies you to serve on the state's highest court?

candidates.name: Theo Angelis
candidates.one_liner: Long-time appellate lawyer appointed by Gov. Ferguson. Institutional and careful. Same coalition as Melody but without the direct civil rights signal.
candidates.prior_practice: Partner at K&L Gates in Seattle for 26 years. Led appellate practice, then global IP. Constitutional, appellate, tribal, real property, and IP litigation.
candidates.endorsements: Sitting WA Supreme Court justices; Gov. Ferguson; former Gov. Gregoire; Democratic officials.

signals.type: Who put him on the court
signals.text: Ferguson appointee. As AG, Ferguson defended the capital gains tax that survived because of how the court read around Culliton.
26-year appellate lawyer. Has written treatise material on Washington law. Knows how appellate doctrine actually moves.

signals.type: How he talks about the law
signals.text: Careful legal analysis and thoughtful decision-making. Moderate, institutionalist tone. Not a textualist self-identification.

signals.type: What he has said about Culliton
signals.text: Nothing directly. No public tax or Article VII record.

questions: How does appellate experience shape your approach to overturning precedent?
What weight do you give to how the legislature has built around a long-standing ruling?

candidates.name: David Larson
candidates.one_liner: Self-described textualist running with anti-income-tax money behind him. His coalition's whole tax politics depends on the 1933 ruling staying put.
candidates.prior_practice: Municipal court bench. Private practice before that. Has run for higher judicial office before.
candidates.endorsements: Conservative-leaning endorsements. Previously backed by Project 42, an anti-income-tax independent expenditure group.

signals.type: How he talks about the law
signals.text: Self-described textualist. Constitution as written framing.
Project 42, an anti-income-tax group, has supported him in prior races.
Anti-income-tax candidates have every reason to protect the 1933 rule because it's the constitutional wall that blocks the income tax their funders oppose.
Municipal court doesn't generate appellate opinions on Article VII. Signal here is methodology and funding, not record.

questions: If you concluded the 1933 ruling was wrong as a matter of original meaning, what would you do about it?
When the party relying on a precedent is the state government itself, how should that change your analysis?

candidates.name: Sharonda Amamilo
candidates.one_liner: Former public defender. Pursuing Democratic endorsements but talks more about due process than tax policy. Genuine question mark on this issue.
candidates.endorsements: Pursuing Democratic LD endorsements; bar ratings pending.

signals.text: Public defender career. Civil-liberties orientation.

signals.type: How she talks about the law
signals.text: Her stare decisis framing leans more formal-conservative than reformist.
Democratic-aligned endorsements with a procedural-justice frame, not a tax-policy one.
Nothing on Article VII or Culliton. Genuine uncertainty.

questions: What are the most important factors when deciding whether to overturn a precedent?
Does the case for overturning weigh differently when the party who has relied on the ruling is the legislature itself?

candidates.name: Debra Stephens
candidates.one_liner: She literally wrote the opinion that upheld the capital gains tax without touching Culliton. She had the chance to revisit the 1933 ruling and chose not to.
candidates.prior_practice: Court of Appeals (Division Three); academic and appellate-litigation background.
candidates.endorsements: Broad bipartisan judicial and bar-association endorsements typical of an incumbent Chief Justice.

signals.text: Wrote the lead opinion in Quinn v. State (2023), which upheld the capital gains tax — but she did it by calling it an excise tax, not by overturning the 1933 ruling. She explicitly declined to revisit Culliton.

signals.type: How she talks about the law
signals.text: Institutionalist. Her approach in Quinn was to work around the old precedent rather than confront it.
Public writings and CLE talks emphasize careful statutory construction, judicial restraint, and respect for precedent.
If a graduated income tax — clearly framed as a tax on income — reached the court, Stephens has signaled she would not be the vote to overturn Culliton. She might find a workaround instead.

questions: When you wrote the Quinn opinion, what made you decide not to revisit the 1933 ruling?
Under what circumstances would the court be justified in overturning a tax precedent that old?
